original_hypothesis.title: Pagination Bug in `getPositionsForCurator` Causes Reverts and Data Corruption
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `getPositionsForCurator` function uses the loop variable `i` (which starts at `start_`) to index into the newly allocated `credIds` and `amounts` arrays (which are sized `stopIndex - start_`). If `start_ > 0`, `i` will exceed the bounds of the arrays, causing a revert.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Incorrect array indexing in view functions breaks integrations and frontends that rely on pagination to read user state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: credIds[i] = credId; amounts[i] = amount; where i can be >= credIds.length

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Using global loop index for a locally sized array
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The array is sized `stopIndex - start_`, but accessed at index `i` which starts at `start_`
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use the `index` variable for array assignment

analysis_questions: Does the function revert for all paginated calls?
Does the assembly truncation at the end discard valid data when `start_ == 0` but entries are skipped?
validation_requirements: Verify the call reverts with an out-of-bounds error

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A frontend or external smart contract attempts to paginate a user's large portfolio of credentials by calling `getPositionsForCurator(user, 10, 20)`. The call reverts due to an array out-of-bounds error, breaking the integration and preventing the user from viewing or interacting with their portfolio.
prerequisites: User has enough positions to require pagination

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but breaks core protocol functionality

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for pagination with start_ > 0

validation_result.mechanism: Out-of-bounds array access and incorrect indexing
validation_result.impact_summary: Calls to `getPositionsForCurator` with `start_ > 0` will revert due to out-of-bounds array access. Additionally, if any entries are skipped, valid data is written to incorrect indices and subsequently truncated, returning zeros.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `getPositionsForCurator` function in `Cred.sol` allocates memory arrays `credIds` and `amounts` with a length of `stopIndex - start_`. However, inside the loop, it uses the loop variable `i` (which iterates from `start_` to `stopIndex - 1`) to assign values to these arrays (`credIds[i] = credId; amounts[i] = amount;`). 

If `start_ > 0`, the index `i` will immediately be out of bounds for the newly allocated arrays, causing a runtime revert. For example, if `start_ = 10` and `stopIndex = 20`, the arrays are sized 10, but the first write is at index 10, which is out of bounds.

Furthermore, even if `start_ == 0`, the function is still defective if any `credId` is skipped (i.e., `_credIdExistsPerAddress[curator_][credId]` is false). In this case, `i` will advance faster than the `index` variable. Valid data will be written to `credIds[i]`, leaving `credIds[index]` as zero. At the end of the function, inline assembly is used to truncate the array length to `index`. Because the valid data was written at indices `>= index`, it gets truncated and lost, while the returned array contains the skipped zeros.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The evidence clearly shows `credIds[i]` and `amounts[i]` being used instead of `credIds[index]` and `amounts[index]`. This directly violates the bounds of the locally sized arrays when `start_ > 0` and corrupts the data layout when entries are skipped.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The arrays `credIds` and `amounts` are sized `stopIndex - start_`. The loop variable `i` starts at `start_`. Assigning to index `i` causes an out-of-bounds revert when `start_ > 0`. It also causes sparse arrays when `_credIdExistsPerAddress` is false, which are then incorrectly truncated by the assembly block at the end of the function.

calling_sequence: Arrays credIds and amounts are allocated with size (stopIndex - start_)
Inside the loop, credIds[i] is accessed, which is >= the array length, causing a revert
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: In `Cred.sol`, `getPositionsForCurator` allocates arrays of size `stopIndex - start_` at lines 335-336. At line 339, a loop starts with `uint256 i = start_`. At lines 343-344, the code assigns values to `credIds[i]` and `amounts[i]`. If `start_ > 0`, `i` is out of bounds. If `start_ == 0` but an item is skipped, `i > index`, leaving zeros at `index` which are then preserved while the actual data at `i` is truncated by the assembly block at lines 349-352.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A frontend application attempts to load a user's portfolio using pagination because the user has many credentials. It calls `getPositionsForCurator(user, 10, 20)`. The call reverts with an out-of-bounds error, preventing the frontend from displaying the user's portfolio. Alternatively, if the frontend calls `getPositionsForCurator(user, 0, 10)` and the user has sold all shares of their 3rd credential (so `_credIdExistsPerAddress` is false), the returned array will contain zeros for the remaining valid credentials, displaying corrupted data to the user.
validation_result.impact_assessment: While this does not lead to direct financial loss, it severely breaks core protocol view functionality. Integrations, frontends, and external smart contracts relying on this function for pagination or portfolio tracking will fail or receive corrupted data, leading to a degraded user experience and potential logic errors in composing contracts.
recommended_mitigations: Change the array assignments inside the loop to use the `index` variable instead of `i`: `credIds[index] = credId; amounts[index] = amount;`.
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Deploy the `Cred` contract.
2. Create a credential and buy shares for a user so they have at least 2 positions.
3. Call `getPositionsForCurator(user, 1, 2)`.
4. Observe the transaction reverting with an out-of-bounds error.
5. Call `getPositionsForCurator(user, 0, 2)` after removing the first position (e.g., by selling all shares so `_credIdExistsPerAddress` becomes false).
6. Observe the returned arrays containing zeros instead of the valid data for the second position.
